summary: The paper argues that reported LLM benchmark scores conflate a model's intrinsic capability with properties of the entire evaluation system — the dataset, the prompting method, the decoding parameters, and the software/hardware stack — so that attributing an observed score to the model alone is unreliable. To address this, the authors propose "LLM evaluatology," a framework built on two layers. A Minimal Evaluation System (MES) fixes ten components spanning dataset, prompting, and decoding (e.g., language, question format, paraphrase, shot count, chain-of-thought, multi-turn, temperature, top-p, presence penalty, and max tokens) and samples the resulting configuration space. An Augmented MES (A-MES) further generates question variants through five automatic pipelines — three analogical (distractor insertion, numeric substitution, conditional recomposition) and two that synthesize genuinely new items (recent-source and conceptual-synthesis) — to help separate memorization from reasoning. Using a design-of-experiments/ANOVA variance decomposition together with a convergence-based stopping rule, the framework quantifies each component's main effect and, notably, its interaction effects. Across nine LLMs on AIME, MMLU, and GPQA, the authors show that a single model's accuracy can vary dramatically across configurations (up to a 70% swing on AIME), that pairwise model rankings reverse, and that most variance is attributable to Question Format, chain-of-thought, and max-tokens, with several interaction effects (e.g., Shot x Multi-Turn) that are jointly significant even when the individual components are not.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
This paper tackles a genuinely important and often-overlooked problem, and its central empirical demonstration is convincing. Showing that a single model's measured accuracy can swing by as much as 70% on AIME and that pairwise superiority conclusions reverse under configuration changes makes concrete a point the community too often treats casually — that benchmark scores are properties of the whole evaluation system rather than of the model in isolation. The audit in Table 1 of which existing benchmarks control which components is a useful reference contribution, and the conclusion-reversal result (Figure 4b) is one of the paper's strongest. The experimental effort is substantial: nine LLMs across roughly 500 configurations on three benchmarks, plus the full A-MES construction pipeline and complete ANOVA tables for every model. I particularly value the interaction-effects analysis, which goes beyond prior work that studies evaluation components in isolation; the finding that some combinations (such as Shot x Multi-Turn) are jointly significant even when neither component is individually significant is a meaningful empirical result. Reproducibility is handled with care through a fixed-seed shuffled ordering and a convergence-plus-law-of-large-numbers stopping criterion, and the three-layer dataset split (seen / analogical / novel) is a useful lens on what a benchmark actually tests.

My main concern is with the soundness of the framing rather than the empirics. The title and repeated use of "causally faithful" promise more than the paper delivers: there is no causal graph, no explicit structural causal model, and no do-calculus or identification argument. What the method actually does is decompose variance via ANOVA over a controlled configuration space, which quantifies contribution and association but does not by itself establish causation. The causal language should either be substantiated with a formal model and its identifying assumptions, or toned down to match what is demonstrated. Relatedly, the statistical inference behind the stopping rule needs tightening: halting sampling once the running-mean accuracy changes by less than 0.002 over the last three updates is a form of sequential peeking, and without anytime-valid or sequential confidence bounds the reported intervals are not formally sound. The proposed "ground truth" — a uniform average over all configurations, including extreme settings such as temperature 2.0 or Arabic prompts on an English math benchmark — also assumes every configuration is equally relevant, which is in tension with the paper's own observation that real deployments cluster around particular settings; the Appendix A.2 ablation excluding extreme settings helps, but a principled deployment-aware weighting is neither proposed nor justified. A further methodological risk is that all five A-MES pipelines use GPT-5 as the executor, so the augmented data may inherit GPT-5's biases and failure modes and could advantage architecturally similar models; a control using a non-GPT transformation model would strengthen the claims, as would a systematic check (human or LLM) of the solvability and semantic consistency of the augmented questions.

On originality and significance, the framework is a sensible integration of sensitivity analysis, data augmentation, and variance decomposition, but the individual ingredients are largely established. The high-level finding that evaluation is fragile and rankings are unstable is already extensively documented (e.g., large accuracy swings from formatting alone, decoding-parameter sensitivity, and ranking instability across many models), and ANOVA-style variance decomposition is a standard tool with recent LLM applications. The distinctive contribution is therefore the joint ten-component MES/A-MES design and the interaction analysis rather than a fundamentally new methodology, and because the work builds directly on the broader Evaluatology framework it at times reads more as an application of that framework than an independent methodological advance. The scope also limits the significance: all three benchmarks have fixed reference answers, so the "benchmark-agnostic" claim is not supported for open-ended generation, preference-based evaluation, or code, and the main text is heavily AIME-centric with MMLU and GPQA deferred to a brief appendix that offers little detailed analysis. Finally, cost and feasibility are under-discussed — running hundreds of configurations per model per benchmark is a large multiplier, yet no API-call or token accounting is reported and no reduced-budget strategy or component prioritization is offered, which matters for whether the approach is usable in routine practice.

Presentation is generally clear but has room to improve. Section 3 (Motivation) substantially repeats the Introduction; the terminology load is high (MES, A-MES, EC, O, C1-C10); some notation in Tables 1 and 5 (e.g., "x" and "y/n") is used without being formally defined; several citations are missing publication years; and the MMLU/GPQA results receive little detailed treatment. A few of the causal-faithfulness definitions would also benefit from intuitive worked examples to connect the abstract framing to the concrete steps a practitioner would take. The appendix — with pseudocode, flowcharts, worked examples, full ANOVA tables, and the extreme-setting ablation — is a strong point that partly offsets these issues.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. The paper repeatedly uses causal language ("causally faithful"), while the empirical analysis rests on controlled configuration comparisons and ANOVA variance decomposition. What precise causal claim is intended, and what assumptions (and identification arguments) are required for that interpretation to hold?
2. Given that sampling stops once the running-mean accuracy changes by less than 0.002 over the last three updates, are the reported confidence intervals anytime-valid? If not, how are the intervals justified under this sequential stopping procedure?
3. How does A-MES performance correlate with similarity to GPT-5, and have you run a control with a non-GPT transformation model? Relatedly, is there any systematic human or LLM evaluation of the solvability and semantic consistency of the augmented questions?
4. What is the total cost (in dollars or tokens, and API-call counts) of running the full MES and A-MES evaluation for one model on one benchmark, and is there a minimum-viable or reduced-budget version that prioritizes the most influential components for resource-constrained settings?
5. How would the framework handle benchmarks without fixed reference answers — open-ended generation, preference-based evaluation, or code — where "accuracy" is not well-defined? A concrete proposal, even if not fully implemented, would help support the benchmark-agnostic claim.
limitations: The limitations discussion is only partial and would benefit from a dedicated section. In particular, the authors should explicitly address the AIME-centric scope (with MMLU and GPQA largely deferred to the appendix), the absence of any cost/feasibility accounting, the reliance of A-MES on GPT-5 as the single transformation model, and the gap between the uniformly averaged "ground truth" and realistic deployment usage patterns.
